policy_statement: Electrify 90% of total existing City-owned buildings by 2035.
mechanism_description: Electrifying existing buildings replaces fossil fuel end uses with electric equipment, reducing direct building combustion emissions (and overall emissions as the grid decarbonizes).
typology_evidence_quote: "Electrify 90% of total existing City-owned buildings by 2035."
primary_causal_pathway: Fossil fuel end-use in buildings → electrification (fuel switching) → ↓ on-site combustion + (with cleaner grid) ↓ lifecycle energy emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Building design, construction, and retrofit decisions shift end-uses (space heating, water heating, cooking, sometimes clothes drying) from gas/oil/propane to electric technologies (e.g., heat pumps, induction, electric resistance in limited cases).  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced direct combustion of fossil fuels in buildings lowers CO₂ (and some methane-related leakage/combustion byproducts); upstream electricity emissions depend on grid mix and decline as the grid decarbonizes.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in building-sector GHG emissions (primarily CO₂, with potential methane co-benefits from reduced gas throughput), lowering overall emissions trajectory.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced operational GHG emissions from buildings by replacing on-site fossil fuel combustion with electricity (increasingly low-carbon over time).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Fuel switching in buildings reduces fossil fuel combustion, leading to lower GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While electrification can improve energy efficiency (a resource-efficiency co-benefit) and can support resilience when paired with storage/microgrids (an adaptation co-benefit), the mechanism as stated is explicitly aimed at emissions reduction from building energy use, which is a mitigation objective and effect.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity supply is highly carbon-intensive in the near term, short-run emissions benefits may be smaller or delayed; however, the dominant intended climate effect across policies using this mechanism remains operational emissions reduction as grids decarbonize.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that “supports electrified renovations and new construction replacing fossil fuel end-uses with electricity” shares the same primary mitigation pathway of reducing combustion-related emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy is best evidenced as primarily code-based, incentive-based, or performance-standard-driven (and the magnitude/timing of benefits) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific design and local grid context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/load growth and peak-demand impacts could increase emissions if marginal generation is fossil-based; benefits depend on grid carbon intensity and implementation details (heat pump quality, envelope improvements). Electrification paired with efficiency upgrades could shift some interpretation toward Resource Efficiency, but the stated dominant mechanism is emissions reduction via fuel switching.
co_benefits: Improved indoor air quality (reduced combustion pollutants); potential resilience benefits during extreme heat if electrification includes efficient cooling/heat pumps; potential reduced methane leakage associated with lower gas use.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a procurement/asset-management requirement for municipal facilities, but the text is framed purely as a quantified goal with a timeframe and no explicit purchasing/retrofit mandate language—so classified as target-setting.
